sources.title: S&P Global, European NATO Debt Projections from Defence Targets
sources.author: S&P Global

nodes.statement: UK defence spending was £60.2 billion in 2024/25 at 2.3% of GDP, with the 2025 NATO Hague Summit agreeing a 5% target by 2035 comprising 3.5% core military plus 1.5% resilience

5% of UK GDP would be approximately £130 billion per year in defence spending, more than double current levels, with S&P Global estimating the 3.5% core target alone could add approximately $2 trillion to European NATO collective debt by 2035
UK armed forces personnel have fallen from approximately 190,000 to approximately 140,000 since 2010, with the armed forces roughly 15% below target strength
Day-to-day defence spending fell 2.4% in real terms since 2015/16 while capital spending rose 95.5%, meaning the military has new equipment but fewer people to operate it
Germany overtook the UK as NATO's second-highest spender in 2024 for the first time, spending $93.7 billion with a 24% increase, while European NATO spending increased 16% in a single year

The OBR estimates reaching 3% of GDP for defence would cost an additional £17.3 billion per year by 2029/30, with the IFS noting non-defence spending is planned to be almost flat due to rapid defence growth

Spain's Prime Minister refused the 5% NATO target, capping at 2.1% and arguing it was disproportionate and incompatible with a welfare state — the most honest statement any ally has made about the trade-off

Labour plans to cut overseas development aid from 0.5% to 0.3% of GNI to fund defence, providing approximately £6.1 billion, despite the military establishment arguing aid spending is itself a security investment
The 2.5% of GDP defence spending target is sufficient for UK security given the current threat environment
nodes.verdict: ALREADY OBSOLETE. NATO agreed 5% at The Hague. 3.5% core is the real minimum for credibility. Defence Select Committee concluded forces must be fully funded or commitments reduced. Germany now outspends the UK.

nodes.statement: Defence spending increases can be funded by cutting overseas development aid without creating new security risks
nodes.verdict: SHORT-TERM FIX THAT MAY WORSEN LONG-TERM SECURITY. £6.1bn from aid provides immediate funding but aid addresses upstream causes of instability — failed states, displacement, poverty — that generate security threats. The military itself has argued aid is a security investment.

nodes.statement: The nuclear deterrent remains the appropriate strategic insurance and should be maintained at current scale
nodes.verdict: STRATEGIC CASE STRONG but FISCAL COST ENORMOUS. Case strengthened by Russian nuclear threats and uncertain US commitment. However nuclear spending crowds out conventional capabilities — fewer ships, aircraft, ground forces than before WWI. Maintain but be honest about opportunity cost.

nodes.statement: The capital-heavy investment bias toward new equipment rather than personnel and readiness is the right balance for UK defence
nodes.verdict: BALANCE IS WRONG. Capital up 95.5% while day-to-day fell 2.4%. Armed forces 15% below strength. Ukraine proves mass still matters alongside technology. Need personnel AND technology, not one at the expense of the other.

nodes.statement: Defence spending can increase to 3.5% of GDP without squeezing other public services or raising taxes
nodes.verdict: NOT WITHOUT TAX INCREASES. IFS says non-defence spending is 'almost flat' due to defence growth. Reaching 3% without tax rises means real cuts to NHS, education, housing, welfare — all already underfunded. Defence increases require taxation reform.

nodes.statement: Spain's framing that 5% of GDP on defence is incompatible with a welfare state is the most honest statement any NATO ally has made, and the UK has not confronted this trade-off

The total additional spending need across all domains — NHS £20-25bn, housing £5-10bn, education £7-12bn, defence £30-70bn — is £80-130 billion per year, while total revenue from taxation reforms is only £17-40 billion, leaving an enormous unfunded gap no party acknowledges
Reach 2.5% of GDP by 2027 and establish a credible pathway to 3.5% core by 2035 per NATO commitment, funded through taxation reform rather than cuts to other underfunded public services
Maintain the nuclear deterrent as strategic insurance given Russian threats and uncertain US commitment, while honestly stating the opportunity cost to conventional military capability
Rebalance defence spending from capital-heavy to include adequate personnel, readiness, and ammunition stockpiles alongside investment in drones, autonomous systems, cyber, and AI
Maintain overseas development aid at 0.5% of GNI rather than cutting it to fund defence, because aid addresses upstream causes of instability that generate downstream security threats
Build a sovereign drone and autonomous systems manufacturing capability, as Ukraine demonstrates cheap drones transform warfare at 1/100th the cost of traditional platforms per engagement
Reach 2.5% by 2030, maintain the nuclear deterrent, pursue AUKUS, deliver new equipment programmes, and introduce National Service for school leavers
Accelerate to 2.5% by 2027 and 3% next parliament via the externally-led Strategic Defence Review, fund partly by cutting aid from 0.5% to 0.3% of GNI, maintain nuclear deterrent and AUKUS
Commit to 2.5% of GDP, review the nuclear deterrent rather than committing to like-for-like Trident replacement, and invest in cyber and resilience alongside maintaining NATO commitment
Set no defence spending target, oppose increases, and redirect military spending to public services and climate action with no mention of NATO targets
Increase military spending with a strong defence posture, introduce National Service, and take a militarised approach to border enforcement
